D. Use the project's estimate at completion (EAC) to show the steering committee the most likely final cost
explanation: Estimate at completion (EAC) is the appropriate earned value forecasting metric to determine the final project cost. EAC is calculated using the formula: Budget at Completion (BAC) divided by Cost Performance Index (CPI). This directly answers the steering committee's question about final project cost. The burndown chart shows feature completion, not financial forecasting, and regression analysis, while potentially useful, is not the standard EVM approach.
topic: Earned Value Management and Budget Forecasting

question: You are working on an agile project where the project sponsor and executive managers require lengthy weekly reports about project progress. This non-value-added work reduces time spent on creating the actual product. What should you do?
options: A. Demonstrate to the customer using visible charts and information radiators
B. Suggest the leaders attend the Sprint review and gather information from the information radiator
C. Ask your project sponsor to wait until a steering committee meeting for the information
D. Share your working project schedule and budget with executives instead of creating reports
explanation: Inviting leaders to the Sprint review and directing them to the information radiator eliminates the need for lengthy reports while providing real-time visibility. An information radiator (big visible chart) contains work completed, remaining tasks, risks, and burn-down data that executives can access anytime. This reduces non-value-added reporting work while maintaining transparency. Option A is similar but the Sprint review plus information radiator is the most complete solution.

question: You are working on a project that has planned extensively up front and would like to transition to a more agile way of working. Currently, the work is hidden and the team meets only once a week in a working group, making it difficult to see what each person is working on. What should you do?
options: A. Ask your team to report on their work in a written status update each day
B. Have more working group meetings so you are always up to date on what the team is working on
C. Ask the team to move their work to a central visible task board such as a Kanban board and hold a short daily standup meeting to discuss progress
D. Remove the team meetings altogether and trust your team to get the work done
explanation: A Kanban board combined with daily standup meetings creates visibility and transparency while improving communication cadence. The standup is a 15-minute daily ceremony where the team discusses progress and identifies blockers. This approach directly addresses the visibility problem and is a core agile practice. Written status updates remain non-value-added work, and removing meetings entirely eliminates necessary collaboration.

question: You are working on a hybrid project where most work was planned upfront and the team manages work via a Kanban board with daily standups. Stakeholders have continuously added scope, and one executive now requests an urgent feature. What should you do?
options: A. Limit the number of cards in the ready column and ask the product owner to prioritize the new item, noting that an existing item may need to be swapped out
B. Match the number of user stories to the team's velocity to ensure a sustainable pace
C. Work with the stakeholder to raise a change request and note the outcome in a change log
D. Review the impact of the scope change with the stakeholder using the work breakdown structure dictionary
explanation: Work-in-progress limits on the Kanban board combined with prioritization is the appropriate approach for managing continuous scope additions in a hybrid environment. The product owner must work with stakeholders to understand that adding new features requires deprioritizing existing items, maintaining a sustainable pace. This uses the pull-based nature of Kanban rather than pushing unlimited work. Change requests and WBS analysis are less appropriate for ongoing agile prioritization decisions.

question: You are working as a product owner on an agile team where the main measure of project success is the number of features delivered into production. Over the past 6 months, your team has delivered the highest number of features your organization has ever seen. However, customers are now complaining about issues and bugs in the product. What should you do?
options: A. Ask the team to work harder to deliver more features and fix product issues
B. Adjust the product metrics to include customer satisfaction and quality measures along with feature delivery
C. Add a slack card of five points in the Sprint backlog to deal with technical debt
D. Implement a code review process to catch bugs before they reach production
explanation: Adjusting project metrics to include quality and customer satisfaction measures will change team behavior and focus. Teams naturally optimize for what is measured; by expanding success criteria beyond feature count to include quality and customer happiness, the team will balance speed with quality. While addressing technical debt (option C) is also important, changing the metrics addresses the root cause of the problem by redefining what success means.

question: You have been working on a project for the past 8 months. Your project team has been approached by multiple stakeholders for reporting on various performance measures about the project and the product area. A large amount of their time is spent on creating these reports for others, and they have escalated this issue to you. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the project stakeholders to stop interacting with your team
B. Spend a portion of your project budget to automate the reports for your stakeholders
C. Work with the team including the stakeholders to only measure things that will facilitate a decision, help avoid an issue, or increase project performance
D. Invite your stakeholders to your Sprint reviews so they can see a working feature in practice
explanation: The best answer is C because it addresses the root cause by ensuring that only meaningful measurements are collected and reported—those that facilitate decisions, help avoid issues, or improve performance. This approach maintains stakeholder engagement and collaboration while reducing unnecessary reporting burden. Answers A, B, and D may provide some relief but don't solve the core issue of measuring only what matters.
topic: Measurement and Reporting, Team Capacity Management

question: You are working on a project where there is a significant amount of uncertainty about the requirements. There is also a legislative change coming and no one knows what it will mean for your industry. As a project manager, how do you deal with this uncertainty?
options: A. Perform additional planning for your project to improve your view on scope management
B. Hold additional retrospectives to capture challenges and improvements from your team
C. Create a risk assessment of all possible project risks with their risk ratings and owners
D. Gather information and prepare for multiple outcomes and build team resilience through processes and training
explanation: The best answer is D because it comprehensively addresses uncertainty by gathering information to reduce unknowns, preparing for multiple possible outcomes, and building team resilience so team members can effectively work through uncertain conditions. This approach tackles uncertainty from multiple angles. While C addresses risk, it doesn't fully address the broader uncertainty and team resilience aspects that are critical when dealing with general uncertainty.

question: You are working on a project with a vendor where there is significant ambiguity involved. Multiple options are possible that have varying degrees of impact. How will you improve the situation?
options: A. Use experiments and prototypes and Progressive elaboration to work your way through it
B. Ask your team for additional reporting around different options so you can make an informed decision
C. Add all the different options into your project scope so all your bases are covered
D. Work more closely with your senior users so they can inform you of the product requirements
explanation: The best answer is A because it directly addresses situational ambiguity by using Progressive elaboration and prototypes to work through multiple outcomes iteratively, gathering feedback and refining understanding as you go. This approach allows the team to explore different options in a controlled way rather than committing to all options or simply gathering more information. Experiments and prototypes reduce ambiguity through practical testing.

question: You are working on a project to improve software that has been in production for over 17 years and has been modified extensively over that time. It also integrates with many other systems within your organization. You cannot decide which features to deliver first. How will you deal with the complexity?
options: A. List out the possible features you can deliver and prioritize them by cost over benefit
B. Simulate decoupling connecting parts to simplify the number of variables
C. Reframe your view by brainstorming with a diverse set of stakeholders
D. Provide an incentive for your project team for whoever can solve this complex problem
explanation: The best answer is B because simulation of decoupling allows you to reduce complexity by examining what happens when you separate interconnected systems, without physically implementing changes. This systems-based approach helps identify which dependencies are critical and which can be managed separately. While A, C, and D have merit, they don't directly address the systemic complexity caused by numerous interconnections.

question: You are working on a project that requires a very particular programming skill set that is not very common. It is also a very hot job market and the industry has seen in two decades, skilled people are hard to come by and they leave often. The programming language is also evolving at a quick pace. What will you do next?
options: A. Develop the product from scratch in a more common programming language
B. Ask your whole team to learn the programming skill set to cover the risk
C. Use alternatives analysis and ensure a sufficient cost reserve to help complete your project
D. Raise a risk to your project completion with the threat of project team member skill sets
explanation: The best answer is C because it addresses volatility through a pragmatic, mature approach by exploring alternative options and maintaining a cost reserve for contingencies. This dual strategy prepares the project for multiple possible scenarios—such as team member turnover or skill gaps—while keeping flexibility and resources available. While D is also good practice, C provides a more comprehensive response to the volatile conditions described.

question: Your project team has found a significant data vulnerability in one of the features planned in your product backlog where customer data is exposed without password access. The feature is partially made by a third party vendor, and the relationship is owned by a functional manager in your organization. What will you do next?
options: A. Avoid the risk and wait for the third party vendor to fix the vulnerability
B. Transfer the risk by asking a different vendor to create a feature with no vulnerability in their design
C. Escalate the risk to the manager with the appropriate authority to act on the response
D. Accept the risk and move on to other features
explanation: Escalation is appropriate when the project team agrees that the threat is outside the scope of the project or exceeds the project manager's authority. In this case, the risk is severe and owned by a functional manager with a third party vendor, so it is outside the project manager's realm of authority and should be escalated to those who have the authority and information to address it.

question: You have brainstormed risk responses with your project team and project sponsor and decided that you cannot transfer a certain high impact risk. You also cannot avoid the risk and there is no one in the organization to escalate it to. Your project sponsor agrees that you should actively accept the risk. What will you do next?
options: A. Develop a contingency plan that would be triggered if the event occurred
B. Shift the ownership to a third party to manage the risk and bear the impact if it occurs
C. Act to eliminate the threat or protect the project from its impact
D. Take action to reduce the probability of its occurrence and the impact of the threat
explanation: Actively accepting a risk can include developing a contingency plan which would be triggered if the event occurred. This differs from passive acceptance, which means just accepting the risk and doing nothing. The other options represent different response strategies: eliminating the threat is avoiding, reducing probability and impact is mitigating, and shifting ownership is transferring.
topic: Risk Response Planning - Active Acceptance and Contingency Plans

question: You are working on a project to build a new customer database system for an engineering team. While working with the requirements, you realize that this system can also work with other teams in your organization and the effort to add those teams is quite small. What will you do next?
options: A. Escalate the opportunity to senior management
B. Exploit the opportunity to actively ensure it occurs
C. Enhance the opportunity by increasing the probability of its occurrence
D. Actively accept the opportunity and acknowledge its existence
explanation: Exploit is the appropriate response to opportunities when acting to ensure the opportunity occurs. Escalation is for opportunities outside the project manager's authority, enhancement is for increasing probability of occurrence, and active acceptance merely acknowledges the opportunity without taking action. Exploiting the opportunity means taking direct action to make sure it happens.

question: You are working as a product owner in an agile team. During a recent risk review, the team has uncovered various risks that will impact your project. You brainstorm risk responses with your team, and the project customer would like them to be mitigated as soon as possible. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to work on the risk responses and get them done as soon as possible
B. Ignore the risks initially as some might dissipate over time and only the important ones will be left
C. Assign the risk responses to all stakeholders around your project team to share the load
D. Prioritize the risks by comparing the expected monetary value of the risk to the anticipated return on investment
explanation: Prioritizing risk responses in a risk-adjusted backlog by comparing the expected monetary value of a risk to the anticipated return on investment of a deliverable allows product owners to incorporate risk responses into planned work in a normal way. This ensures that risks are addressed appropriately within the context of overall project priorities and is especially important in agile environments where work is prioritized continuously.
topic: Risk Response Planning in Agile - Prioritization

question: You are planning a project where supply chains have been impacted and the requirements are complex and intertwined with other areas of the business. Your project sponsor asks you to manage these risks before the project moves to execution. What will you do next?
options: A. Move to project execution so you can work through risks as they happen
B. Move to an agile way of work so you can deliver faster and deal with the complexity
C. Note the risks with appropriate controls and add a contingency reserve in the project budget
D. Assign a management reserve to address risks
explanation: Before moving to project execution, you should identify risks and establish appropriate controls with a contingency reserve in the project budget to account for identified risks should they occur. A management reserve is used for unknown events such as unplanned in-scope work, not for identified risks. This approach follows the standard practice of risk quantification and planning before execution.
topic: Risk Response Planning - Contingency and Management Reserves

question: How do you manage risk on an agile project?
options: A. Hold a risk meeting but only with the immediate project team to avoid unnecessary participants

B. Use daily standups to report blockers early, hold fortnightly demonstrations to catch stakeholder dissatisfaction, use the kanban board to see work flow, and recognize that risk review can surface threats and opportunities through retrospectives
C. There is no need for a separate meeting as risk is everyone's responsibility in an agile team
D. Use only the kanban board to visualize work and uncover risks
explanation: In agile teams, risk review takes multiple forms including daily standups for reporting blockers that could become threats, frequent product demonstrations to surface threats and opportunities, and retrospectives. While quality is everyone's responsibility, the approach integrates risk management throughout agile ceremonies rather than in a separate meeting.

question: Your project management office has no prescribed way of work and is working with a vendor who supplies an extreme programming framework at a significant cost. Projects are still running behind schedule, over budget, and with low customer satisfaction. What should you do?
options: A. Tailor the approach to suit the organization's operating environment and project needs
B. Use a waterfall approach instead to ensure the proper planning
C. Switch to scrum instead since it is a more popular framework
D. Tighten controls around the project environment with a more directive PMO approach
explanation: Projects operate in complex environments with competing demands. Tailoring is performed to better suit the organization's operating environment and project needs. Simply switching frameworks or tightening controls without tailoring to the specific context will not address the root issues.

question: Your project management office is in the middle of a company-wide ways of working transformation. They have taken a popular project framework and are tailoring it to the organization with branded templates and processes that meet their existing governance requirements. You have been asked to help finalize the process. What will you do next?
options: A. Gather feedback from the functional business areas to ensure the process meets their needs
B. Roll out the new way of working to the PMO as a pilot, then to the rest of the organization
C. Enable a process for tailoring based on size and complexity and implement ongoing improvement
D. Form a working group comprised of executives and project professionals
explanation: The tailoring process includes: select the initial development approach, tailor for the organization, tailor for the project, and implement ongoing improvement. This answer encompasses both enabling tailoring flexibility and the continuous improvement cycle needed for organizational change.

question: You are a manager of a PMO in a large organization that has recently undertaken a company-wide way of working transformation. You need to select an initial development approach for your project, which you will then tailor further. What will you do next?
options: A. Use a framework that best engages your executive team through easy-to-use graphics and presentations
B. Select the most popular current framework to keep up with what other organizations are doing
C. Select multiple development approaches as a baseline for your organization
D. Use a suitability filter to assess the most appropriate development approach
explanation: A suitability filter helps project teams consider whether a project has characteristics that lend themselves towards a predictive, hybrid, or adaptive approach. You then select one as the base and enable the ability to tailor to your project or organization's circumstances, rather than being influenced by popular frameworks or executive preferences.

question: Your organization is starting to move to an agile way of work and is setting up a value delivery office (VDO) to complement the existing PMO. Executives have begun working through what the function looks like and what roles and responsibilities might exist. They approach you for help. What will you tell them?
options: A. The VDO is responsible for the value delivered and owns the programs within the organization
B. The VDO focuses on coaching project teams, building adaptive skills, growing organizational capabilities, and mentoring sponsors and product owners to be more effective
C. The VDO is responsible for agile resources such as scrum masters and product owners to be allocated effectively to project teams
D. The VDO creates the organizational framework to be used on each project throughout the company
explanation: A VDO serves an enabling role rather than a management or oversight function. It focuses on coaching project teams, building adaptive skills and capabilities throughout the organization, and mentoring sponsors and product owners. This is distinct from the PMO's more directive governance function.

question: You have been planning a project for the last two months, gathering requirements and scope and assigning tasks to a schedule. As you get closer to the execution phase, you need to decide what delivery approach you will use. The requirements are likely to change, require a medium amount of compliance, but can be released in one go. What will you do next?
options: A. Use a predictive approach
B. Use an incremental approach
C. Use an adaptive agile approach
D. Use an iterative approach
explanation: Gathering feedback and improving over time while delivering in one release is an iterative delivery approach. Incremental releases increments of value like features; predictive plans everything upfront and delivers in one go; and agile gathers feedback and releases incrementally. The scenario calls for iteration toward a single release.

question: You have been asked to make a process improvement in the accounting area of your organization. You have been assigned a small team of nine people, each with all the skills you need to complete the project. The team is collocated and has unlimited access to the project customer. How will you tailor your delivery approach?
options: A. Use a predictive approach
B. Use an agile or adaptive delivery approach
C. Use an incremental approach
D. Use a hybrid delivery approach
explanation: An agile or adaptive delivery approach works best when the team is small (fewer than 9 to 12 people), has close access to the customer, is cross-functional with all required skills, and is collocated in the same space. These conditions create the ideal environment for agile delivery.

question: You are working on an agile project and have noticed that things are not working well and could be improved as you begin new projects. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the product backlog to see the flow of features going through the teams
B. Look into issues, threats, and quality assurance statistics to identify defects
C. Hold a retrospective with your PMO leaders to determine ongoing improvement opportunities
D. Review a control chart and value stream map to see non-value-added activities and remove waste
explanation: In an agile environment, holding a retrospective is the best way to gather feedback directly from the team on what is working well and what could be improved. While control charts and value stream mapping (option D) are also valid problem-solving approaches, the retrospective is the most direct method for getting broad input and identifying solutions as a team. This approach is referenced in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 151, under tailoring diagnostics for agile teams.

question: You have been working on a project to deliver a new customer data system and have noticed that the work is starting to slow down with fewer user stories appearing in your sprint backlog as they are not ready. After holding a retrospective, you discovered that there are long delays waiting for requirements and acceptance criteria approvals. What will you do next?
options: A. Streamline approval decisions through fewer people, such as a product owner authorized to make decisions up to certain value thresholds
B. Ask your team to place the user stories in the sprint backlog, reducing the need for customer approval
C. Note the long delays as a risk to your project with high likelihood since it is already happening
D. Escalate the issue to the functional manager to ensure approvals are happening quickly
explanation: The best approach is to streamline decision-making by empowering fewer people to make approvals up to certain value thresholds. This removes bottlenecks in the approval process without bypassing customer involvement (which option B would do). Escalating to a functional manager does not solve the root problem. This tailoring suggestion is directly from the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 151, under common situations and tailoring suggestions for agile environments.

question: You have been assigned to take over an existing project as the previous project manager leaves for another opportunity. In working with your team, you notice that there is a large amount of work currently in progress at once, with team members working on multiple items at a time and constantly context switching between tasks. What will you do next?
options: A. Add more people to the project to handle the workload
B. Use value stream mapping and kanban boards to visualize the work and brainstorm solutions with your team
C. Ask your team to work overtime or to cover the extra work and bring it all back in line
D. Review the scope of the project and reduce items with low customer impact
explanation: Using value stream mapping and kanban boards allows you to visualize the work in progress and implement limiting mechanisms (such as WIP limits) to reduce context switching. Adding more people complicates communication and processes. Working overtime is not sustainable and ruins workflow. While reviewing scope (option D) is good practice, the best answer directly addresses the root cause of the problem—too much work in progress. This is recommended in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 151.
topic: Kanban, Work in Progress Limits, and Flow Management

question: The product owner has prioritized the product backlog of features and the team creates user stories with estimates and places enough of them to fill a two-week sprint. You notice that the pieces being completed are being rejected by the customer due to defects and missed requirements. What will you do next?
options: A. Create the project scope from scratch with the project team and customers
B. Have an iteration zero planning sprint with your project team to set it up for success
C. Add more feedback loops such as shoulder checks, peer review, unit testing, and system testing
D. Have less frequent customer demonstrations so they only see the product when it is correct
explanation: Adding more feedback loops and quality assurance steps at all levels—including demonstrations, reviews, peer reviews, shoulder checks, pair programming, unit testing, system testing, user acceptance testing, regression testing, and automated testing—allows you to catch defects and missed requirements earlier when they are less costly to fix. Creating scope from scratch (option A) is too drastic. Iteration zero (option B) delays actual work unnecessarily. Reducing customer demonstrations (option D) removes the critical feedback loop needed for agile success. This approach is recommended in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 151.
topic: Quality Assurance and Feedback Loops in Agile

question: Your project team has started to find their rhythm and is working well together. However, you notice that they seem disengaged and are dragging their feet on normal tasks. You check your project budget and note that the team are all paid fairly for the market and within the same range for the role. What will you do next to improve engagement?
options: A. Look for ways to reward the team members with bonuses above their normal pay rate
B. Move team members around to different roles frequently so they experience job variety
C. Give the team a paid day off
D. Establish ways for the team to grow and advance
explanation: While pay is important, once it is fair, engagement comes from other factors such as a sense of progress, career development, and growth opportunities. Research by Frederick Herzberg identified that hygiene factors like pay and working conditions must be adequate, but true motivation comes from growth, advancement, and a sense of progress. Bonuses (option A) provide only temporary relief and wear off quickly. Moving team members frequently (option B) disrupts expertise and flow. A paid day off (option C) provides temporary relief but does not address underlying engagement issues. Establishing growth pathways creates sustained motivation through progress and advancement.
topic: Team Motivation and Herzberg's Motivation-Hygiene Theory

question: You are working as a project manager on a regulatory change for your organization. You notice that the team are not communicating as well as they could be and you put together a plan to improve team communication by increasing its effectiveness and richness. What will you do?
options: A. Include methods that will handle multiple information cues such as body language, voice, and words combined to give you the message
B. Facilitate rapid feedback to ensure everyone is on the same page
C. Utilize natural language so that none of this jargon or fancy other language is used
D. Include ways to get sign off in writing so it can be recorded and reviewed later when needed
explanation: According to Alistair Cockburn's Crystal methodology, media richness and effectiveness of communication channels is best achieved by handling multiple information cues (body language, voice, words), enabling rapid feedback, establishing personal focus, and utilizing natural language. Answer A directly addresses the core principle of effectiveness and richness by including methods to handle multiple information cues, which are the foundational elements of the Crystal framework for communication effectiveness.

question: Your deliverables have been signed off by the product owner and they are ready to be released to the customer, which is the financial analytics team of your organization. The new system will be quite a change from their normal day-to-day work. The team already have the desire to use the new system. What will you do next?
options: A. Develop a vision and explain the why behind the change
B. Use training and education to show them the new processes and systems
C. Give them unlimited hands-on practice so they can get used to the new system
D. Include rewards, recognition, feedback, and measurement once the system is released
explanation: According to the ADCAR model for change management (Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement), the sequence of steps is critical. Since the team already has the desire for change, the next step is knowledge, which includes training and education for the new processes and systems. Answer A would be premature since they already have desire. Answer C (hands-on practice) comes after knowledge. Answer D (rewards and recognition) comes during the reinforcement phase.

question: You are leading a large organizational change that affects more than a thousand people across multiple teams. You have engaged with senior managers of that area, created a brief vision statement that summarizes the change, and created a strategy to realize the vision. You communicate the vision throughout the change process. What will you do next?
options: A. Release multiple usable features to the affected teams so they can get used to the change gradually
B. Anchor the changes into corporate culture so we can tell success stories and recognize people
C. Address any obstacles to the change, including outdated processes and people resistance
D. Set goals for continued improvement on the change throughout the organization
explanation: According to Kotter's eight-step process for leading change, the sequence is: 1) Create urgency, 2) Form a coalition, 3) Create a vision, 4) Communicate the vision, 5) Remove obstacles, 6) Create short-term wins, 7) Build on the change, 8) Anchor in corporate culture. Since you have already communicated the vision, the next step is to remove obstacles. Anchoring in culture (B) comes last. Short-term wins (A) and goals for improvement (D) come after removing obstacles.
topic: Kotter's Eight-Step Process for Leading Change

question: You are a project manager assessing the risk of future releases with your team. You have a clear set of requirements from your customer, however there are a range of possible correct solutions. There are a range of known unknowns. How are you going to approach this situation?
options: A. Use current best practices to make decisions
B. Assess the facts, analyze the situation, and apply known good practices
C. Use emergent practices that will allow you to probe the environment, sense the situation, and respond with action
D. Sense where there is some stability and take steps to try and stabilize the situation
explanation: This scenario describes the Cynefin framework's 'Complicated' domain, characterized by known unknowns and a range of correct answers. In the Complicated domain, the appropriate approach is to assess the facts, analyze the situation, and apply good practices. Answer C (emergent practices and probing) applies to the Complex domain (unknown unknowns). Answer D (stabilizing) applies to the Chaotic domain. Answer A (best practices alone without analysis) is insufficient for complicated situations.

question: You have been asked to initiate a project to deliver a new system. The requirements are not clear and it will take some time to uncover them. The system has been used by some team members but not all, and it has a medium level of uncertainty around it. How are you going to approach this project?
options: A. Release small usable features and improve your approach based on feedback
B. Do additional planning up front to ensure every component is thought of
C. Split the team into two and work on different parts of the system to simplify it
D. Form a business case for starting from scratch with new, easier-to-use technology
explanation: According to the Stacy Matrix, which assesses uncertainty in requirements and technology, this project falls in the 'Complex' domain due to medium uncertainty. In complex environments, agile methodologies work best, utilizing iterative delivery of small usable features and responding to feedback to progressively clarify requirements. Answer B would not help reduce uncertainty quickly. Answer C fragments the team. Answer D is a major overhaul that delays value delivery.

question: Your project team came together from different parts of the organization and were introduced formally to each other. You notice that there is some conflict in the team. Some people do not respect others' decisions and are not working together well, and there are some personality clashes within the team. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Help team members get to know each other's names
B. Help the team understand their roles and responsibilities and form a regular rhythm of meetings, collaboration, and work
C. Help the team jockey for position within the team and foster healthy conflict
D. Help the team complete project work and disperse onto other things
explanation: According to Tuckman's stages of team development (Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing), the team is in the Storming phase, characterized by conflict and jockeying for position. The appropriate intervention is to help establish clarity around roles, responsibilities, and working rhythms to move toward the Norming phase. Answer A is too basic for this stage. Answer C would encourage unhealthy competition rather than resolve conflict. Answer D represents the final Adjourning phase, not the current need.

question: You have formed a new project team and have worked through the purpose and mission of the project with them. You expanded on who is on the project team and the skills and abilities each person brings. You would like to foster a high-performing culture and ensure the team is able to deliver as soon as possible. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Work through changes on the project team and the project such as deliverables and stakeholders
B. Decompose the high-level plan into greater detail including a detailed schedule or backlog
C. Define a plan to achieve the project goals including milestone schedules, release plans, and high-level budgets
D. Clarify the goals of the team and what you are here to deliver
explanation: After establishing the vision (purpose and mission) and identifying the team members (who), the next step in building a high-performing culture is to clarify the goals and what the team is here to deliver (the what). This aligns with the Drexler Sibbet Team Performance Model, which includes the steps of orientation, trust building, goal clarification, commitment, implementation, high performance, and renewal. Goal clarification comes after trust building.

question: Your team members have raised an issue as they believe the current project scope will not reach the intended project goal of increasing customer attention. You take their concerns to the project sponsor who controls the funding and resources. The project sponsor dismisses them and directs you to proceed with the scope currently set. What will you do next?
options: A. Problem solve with the sponsor and find a resolution
B. Compromise with the sponsor so each party gets a little bit of what they want
C. Accommodate the sponsor to maintain harmony and goodwill
D. Force the issue so that the sponsor accepts the change
explanation: According to the Thomas-Kilman conflict resolution model, since the sponsor has significant power and authority (they control funding and resources), an accommodating approach is appropriate to maintain the important relationship with the sponsor. While problem-solving (win-win) is ideal, accommodating (lose-win from your perspective) is the most suitable response given the power differential. Compromising would be lose-lose, and forcing would be win-lose, potentially damaging your career relationship with the sponsor.

question: The functional manager raises a concern that they have not been engaged correctly and the program is missing some key requirements as a result. You believe you have captured all the items required. Your program manager asks you to handle the situation and approach it from a win-win perspective. What will you do next?
options: A. Escalate the decision to the steering committee to ensure your point of view is heard
B. Demonstrate integrity, find mutual trust, and look at the situation from their point of view
C. Choose to give some leeway in your approach so the functional manager can add their requirements
D. Get your requirements baselined first and then compete with the manager to ensure a win
explanation: A win-win approach, as described in Stephen Covey's The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People, requires mature character, trust, and the ability to approach the situation from the other person's point of view. Demonstrating integrity, finding mutual trust, and understanding their perspective are the essential elements for achieving a true win-win resolution rather than simply accommodating their demands or competing for victory.

question: You have been planning a project to deliver a process change for the past four weeks. The requirements and scope have been signed off by the business owner and the team are moving into delivery and execution. The work begins in line with the project plan and scope. What will you do next?
options: A. Monitor and control the work to ensure it stays on track and meets its goals
B. Execute the project work
C. Plan the work
D. Formally sign off on the project
explanation: Following the project management process groups—initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and closing—once execution begins, the next critical activity is monitoring and controlling. This ensures the work stays aligned with the plan, meets quality standards, and addresses any issues or changes. Formal sign-off cannot occur until the project is complete.

question: You are working as a product owner. While your sales and revenue have been increasing over the past eight months, the overall profit has decreased by 17 percent. Your executive manager asks you to address the situation. What should you do next?
options: A. Stop the current work and set a planning session with your team to address the situation
B. Ask your team to work overtime on the current features in your backlog to get the work done
C. Perform benchmarking with a similar yet non-competitive company and add any process improvements to your product backlog
D. Look for opportunities to partner with other projects and products in your organization to increase sales further
explanation: The root cause of the profit decline is increased costs to produce or sell the product, not insufficient sales. Performing benchmarking to identify gaps in processes and implementing those improvements will reduce costs and improve profitability. Stopping work (A) is disruptive, working overtime (B) is unsustainable, and increasing sales further (D) does not address the cost problem. As a product owner, responsibility includes increasing profit by managing costs efficiently.
topic: Product Owner Responsibilities and Business Performance

question: You are creating a business case and a project charter with a small initial project team. You have a high-level feature decomposed into smaller release milestones and you'd like to note a high-level schedule using wideband Delphi or a high-level version of planning poker. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Hold a round of cards with points on them where each team member places their cards in the center for the total estimate
B. Ask subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates individually, compare and explain the highest and lowest estimates for each round
C. Show the wideband high-level range from the lowest to highest for each feature
D. Find the business functionality for each feature and estimate the effort based on that
explanation: The wideband Delphi technique asks subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates individually and compare and explain the highest and lowest estimates for each round. Planning poker is a variation of wideband Delphi. Relative estimating with a specific range shown (option C) is not the correct process for wideband Delphi.

question: You have been asked to help facilitate a senior planning meeting in your organization. High-level executives have made a presentation on the strengths and opportunities of the organization, painting a rosy picture. However, revenue has fallen recently and the chief executive asks you to level out their analysis with a more complete view. What will you do next?
options: A. Add your organization's competitors and compare their products
B. Showcase the pipeline of upcoming work to be delivered in the next quarter
C. Add the background and abilities of the executive team that will allow the organization to deliver
D. Brainstorm and capture the weaknesses of the organization and threats to the organization
explanation: This question refers to SWOT analysis (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats). Once strengths and opportunities have been captured, a complete picture also involves the weaknesses and threats to provide a balanced view. The other options do not complete the SWOT framework analysis.

question: You are adding an essential feature to your product roadmap. Your team believes they can complete the feature in 6 weeks. However, the feature is quite large and a bit more complicated than normal. One of your lead developers says they worked on a similar feature in another company and it took around 4 months to complete. What will you do next?
options: A. Use relative estimating where the smallest piece of work is assigned as one and everything else is relative to that
B. Use analogous estimating and place the feature with around 4 months to complete so you are a little bit safer
C. Use parametric estimating to identify parameters such as cost or hours to estimate the feature
D. Use function point estimating to show the amount of business functionality in the feature
explanation: Analogous estimating is appropriate when there are people who have experience delivering a similar item. The lead developer's past experience with a similar feature in another company provides a good basis for analogous estimating, which allows you to estimate effort quickly and with medium-level accuracy. The 4-month estimate provides a valuable reference point compared to the team's 6-week estimate.

question: You have a prioritized backlog of features. You have decomposed the next feature to be delivered into user stories for a 2-week sprint. The team would like to estimate the effort involved on those user stories but are unsure how to proceed. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask each team member to add their own estimates as they complete the acceptance criteria
B. Estimate the effort for the overall feature and reduce the need for individual story estimates
C. Help the team perform relative estimating where the stories are sized in points relative to the smallest piece
D. Base the estimates on cost per hour and add them after the work is done to ensure accuracy
explanation: Relative estimating is the most common method for user stories where they are sized in comparison to the smallest piece, often in story points and frequently on a Fibonacci scale. This method benefits from input from all team members (testers, developers, etc.) and provides velocity insights for sprint planning. Individual estimates (option A) lack the collaborative benefits, and basing estimates on actual hours after completion (option D) is not estimation—it's just recording what happened.
topic: Estimating Techniques - Relative Estimating for User Stories

question: You are halfway through your current 2-week sprint. The work is progressing well. However, you notice that there is not enough work ready and elaborated for when the next sprint begins. What will you do next?
options: A. Hold a backlog refinement meeting and work with the team to identify the work that could be accomplished
B. Raise the issue as a blocker in the daily standup and ask your team to swarm around the problem
C. Raise the issue in the next retrospective and take an action for the team
D. Place the cards on the kanban board so the team can see the work and get started
explanation: At a backlog refinement meeting, the backlog is progressively elaborated and prioritized to identify the work that can be accomplished in an upcoming iteration. Since you are halfway through the current sprint, a backlog refinement meeting is the appropriate action to prepare work for the next sprint. A retrospective (option C) happens at the end of a sprint and would be too late, and swarming (option B) takes people off current work rather than preparing the work properly.

question: You have completed a make or buy analysis in your project and found that part of your deliverable is best created by an external vendor. You work with your project team to create the procurement strategy, source selection criteria, and statement of work. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Gather the seller proposals including technical details and price
B. Meet with prospective sellers to ensure all vendors have a common understanding of the procurement
C. Perform source selection analysis on what you want from prospective vendors
D. Create the procurement agreement for the third party vendor to provide their specified services
explanation: A bidder conference (also called a contractor conference, vendor conference, or pre-bid conference) is a meeting with prospective sellers prior to the preparation of a bid or proposal to ensure all prospective bidders have a clear and common understanding of the procurement. This occurs before gathering proposals and before source selection. This is an output of plan procurement management.

question: One of the stakeholders in your project has raised a change request after some of the deliverables contained defects and a missed requirement. Your project has a formal change control process as opposed to a more agile prioritized product backlog. What will you do next?
options: A. Gather feedback from other stakeholders to see if they also need the change
B. Take it to the change control board
C. Note the change in the change log
D. Raise the request as an issue that will affect your project schedule and budget
explanation: In a formal change control process, change requests must be submitted to the change control board for review, approval, or rejection. The change control board includes the people accountable for reviewing and approving changes to the project. The decisions made are recorded and communicated to stakeholders. This is the proper formal governance process.

question: Your agile project has recently gained some new stakeholders after a department restructure. They complain that they are not seeing enough traction of work delivered through the project and they are worried that it will not meet their requirements. What will you do next?
options: A. Invite them to the daily standup so they can see the work that happens on a daily basis
B. Invite them to iteration planning so they can choose what the team works on
C. Invite them to the retrospective so they can raise their issue there and come up with a solution
D. Invite them to the iteration review so they can see the usable increment that the team delivers
explanation: The iteration review (or sprint review) is held at the end of an iteration to demonstrate the work that was accomplished to the project customers and stakeholders. This allows new stakeholders to see the usable features being delivered and provide feedback. This is the appropriate venue to address their concerns about work being delivered and to gather their input on whether requirements are being met.

question: You have recently taken over a project. Your project team approached you to say that there is a regulatory approval that has not been granted yet and it might block development of your project in the next 5 months. You check your project documentation but cannot see any mention of this. What will you do next?
options: A. Hold a risk review meeting, capture it as a risk, and ensure an adequate risk response
B. Hold a retrospective with your team to capture what challenges the team are facing
C. Adjust your product roadmap to reflect the changes to the timeframe of that deliverable
D. Escalate the regulatory approval to the project steering committee
explanation: A risk review meeting is held to analyze the status of existing risks and identify new risks, including changes to probability, impact, or urgency, and to determine if responses are adequate. Since the regulatory approval has not yet been granted but might block development in the future, this is a potential risk that must be identified, analyzed, and managed with an appropriate response plan. This is more appropriate than escalating or adjusting the roadmap based on an uncertain future event.
topic: Risk Review and Risk Response Planning

question: You are an agile project manager and holding daily standups with your team. You notice people accusing other team members of not doing their job and there are some tasks that are not mentioned anywhere and no one is responsible for. The team velocity has slowed in the last three sprints. What will you do next?
options: A. Refer to the team charter that outlines team roles, agreed ways of managing conflict, and responsibilities
B. Work with your team to problem-solve in the next retrospective and come to a solution with the team
C. Raise it as an issue in the next retrospective
D. Assign team roles to those you think are best suited
explanation: A retrospective is a regularly occurring workshop where the team explores their work to improve their process. Since tasks are not mentioned in the team charter and roles are undefined, the team needs to problem-solve together in a retrospective to clarify responsibilities, resolve conflicts, and improve their process. This is the agile approach to addressing process and team issues.

question: You are managing a large construction project. Due to some delays, the project schedule has been pushed back by one week. As a result, one of the project risks has increased slightly but is still within the acceptable limits. What will you do next?
options: A. Hold a risk review and update the risk response and owners
B. Raise a change request for the change to the project schedule
C. Capture the change in your project reporting and take it to the next status meeting
D. Update the sprint backlog to reflect the delayed work
explanation: Since the risk has increased slightly but is still within acceptable limits, a new risk response may not be necessary. The priority is to communicate the schedule change to stakeholders. A status meeting is a regularly scheduled event to exchange and analyze information about current project progress and performance. Capturing the change in project reporting and communicating it at the status meeting ensures stakeholders are informed of the one-week delay.
topic: Project Status Reporting and Stakeholder Communication

question: As a product owner, you want to measure the effect of the features you are delivering to ensure they are having the desired impact. Which of the following would be the best measure to use?
options: A. Ask them to gather the team velocity and throughput over the past 3 months
B. Ask them to gather the cost benefit ratio over the past 6 months
C. Ask them to gather the NPS over the past 6 months
D. Ask them to gather the average app availability and uptime
explanation: Net Promoter Score (NPS) is a customer measure that gauges overall customer satisfaction and loyalty by asking customers how likely they would be to recommend the product to others on a scale of 1 to 10. Since the product owner represents the customer voice, NPS is the best measure of whether features are having the desired effect on customer satisfaction. Team velocity and throughput are internal team measures, cost-benefit ratio is used for prioritization, and app availability is an operational measure unrelated to feature effectiveness.

question: You are a project manager in an agile team developing new features for an app. An executive stakeholder with high influence in the organization approaches you and asks you to add a feature that they like. They mention that their friends also think it is a good idea. How should you proceed?
options: A. Place the feature in the product backlog and prioritize it using cost versus benefit
B. Raise a change request to add the new feature into the scope baseline
C. Gather the requirements for the new feature and ask your team to elaborate on it
D. Analyze the impact to your project schedule and cost and add the feature to your project scope
explanation: Before committing to work on a feature requested by an influential stakeholder, you should use a prioritization method such as cost versus benefit analysis to evaluate it objectively. This prevents confirmation bias and ensures decisions are based on data rather than the stakeholder's role or title. While you may eventually raise a change request, gather requirements, and analyze impacts, the first step should be to objectively prioritize the feature using a formal method to determine if it should be added to the backlog at all.

question: Your project team is gathering solution ideas for a particular customer requirement which is prioritized to be worked on next. The item is complicated and the team has been trying to find a way forward for the past 3 weeks. There is not enough work for the upcoming sprint. What should you do?
options: A. Start work on the item in the next sprint anyway and elaborate as the sprint begins
B. Arrange the right people to elaborate on the issue with a spike
C. Adjust the project schedule to reflect the extra time taken on elaboration
D. Deprioritize the item until the team can figure out the issue
explanation: A spike is a time-boxed research activity to investigate and resolve a complex requirement. By bringing the right people together and time-boxing the spike to a fixed period (such as 2 days), you create a deadline that prevents endless analysis while still allowing the team to elaborate the requirements before the sprint begins. This keeps work flowing and prevents the team from starting development on fuzzy requirements. Time-boxing is critical to avoid student syndrome and ensure progress.
topic: Agile Requirements Elaboration and Spike Activities

question: Your project team has planned the requirements, scope, schedule, and cost for constructing a new building in your organization. An executive from another area approaches you as your project is funded and about to begin execution and asks if you can help them deliver a website as part of your work. What will you do next?
options: A. Raise the request as an issue in your project's issue log
B. Add the website to your project scope and adjust your project scope, schedule, and cost
C. Raise a change request for the added scope and review the impact to your project schedule and cost
D. Offer to help them create a business case for the new idea and get funding for a new project
explanation: A website project is fundamentally different from a building construction project in terms of scope, team skills, and resource requirements. Rather than adding it to your existing project, the website should be established as a separate project with its own business case, project charter, and funding. A business case is a value proposition for a proposed project that precedes formal approval and kickoff. This prevents scope creep and ensures the website project receives appropriate planning and resources.
topic: Project Scope Management and Business Case Development

question: You are working on an agile project and planning your product backlog for the next 3 to 6 months. You have high-level feature ideas from various parts of your organization, including frontline staff, executives, and direct customer research. What will you do next?
options: A. Break down each feature into epics and user stories and size the work with your team
B. Add all of the items into your product backlog and work on the ones from the managers first
C. Work with the requesters and your technical experts to create a business model canvas for each feature
D. Perform precedence diagramming on each feature to determine their dependencies
explanation: Creating a business model canvas for each feature is an effective way to capture all relevant information on one page, including the value proposition, cost, infrastructure, and customer impact. This information allows you to prioritize the features effectively based on benefits and effort. A business model canvas feeds into subsequent activities like sizing and breaking down features into epics and user stories. Prioritization must come before detailed breakdown and dependency analysis. Working with requesters and technical experts ensures you have the right perspectives to evaluate each feature.
topic: Agile Product Backlog Planning and Business Model Canvas

question: You are working in a new agile team where the work has started to slow and there is uncertainty and low team engagement. You set time to create a team charter with your team and agree on roles, responsibilities, and team cadence. What will you do next to improve team engagement?
options: A. Set a team goal for them to be more engaged in the work over the next 3 months
B. Create a product vision statement showing the value you are bringing and the why behind the work
C. Ask the team to contribute more to all pieces of work during each team meeting
D. Raise it as a people issue for your project and manage it with the project steering committee
explanation: A product vision statement is a critical component of the team charter that articulates the value the team is bringing and the purpose behind the work. This helps teams understand the 'why' behind what they are doing, which directly addresses low engagement and uncertainty. By clarifying the vision and value proposition, team members gain clarity on purpose and motivation. While setting goals, getting broader input, and escalating are options, the product vision statement as part of the team charter is the most direct and effective way to address uncertainty and improve engagement.
topic: Team Charter and Agile Team Engagement

question: You are integrating a third-party system as part of your project. The developers in your project team have not worked on this system before, but they believe the language and layout is the same and they should be able to implement it. Adding a bit of time for the uncertainty, they give a high-level estimate of 5 weeks. What will you do next?
options: A. Note the estimate of 5 weeks and update the Assumption log
B. Don't add the estimate; break down the integration into user stories and use a bottom-up approach
C. Check the estimate using parametric estimating to increase its accuracy
D. Gather your whole team and customers together and estimate using wideband Delphi or planning poker
explanation: The best approach is to note the estimate and document the assumption that the languages and layout are the same. Since the team already has an estimate of 5 weeks and there is a clear technical assumption underlying it, recording this in the Assumption log is the appropriate first step. While the other options (bottom-up estimating, parametric estimating, and wideband Delphi) are all valid estimation techniques, in this scenario where an estimate already exists and is based on a stated assumption, documenting that assumption is the critical next step before proceeding with further refinement.

question: Your agile project has delivered three features so far and is halfway through delivering the latest improvement. During a brainstorming session, one of your customers comes up with a new feature idea. You check the benefit and the benefit is high; the complexity is also low. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Add money to your budget to accommodate the additional feature as soon as possible
B. Elaborate on the feature, break it down into user stories, and estimate them
C. Place the item in the backlog and reprioritize it to be next
D. Create a business case for the new feature outlining the benefit and costs involved
explanation: In an agile project, the scope is variable while budget and time are typically fixed. When a new high-benefit, low-complexity feature is identified, it should be placed in the backlog and reprioritized according to the project's prioritization schema. This is the standard agile process for handling scope changes. While elaborating into user stories is eventually necessary, reprioritization in the backlog comes first. Creating a business case is a waterfall practice, and adding budget unilaterally without going through prioritization is not agile best practice.

question: One of the senior users testing your product has found that it was missing a key requirement that he had assumed would be there. It was not noted in the project scope, which has been baselined already. You check the impact to cost and schedule, raise a change request on his behalf, and it is approved by the Change Control Board. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to elaborate and turn their requirement into user stories
B. Place the new requirement into the Sprint backlog for the next Sprint
C. Add the missing requirement to the issue log and problem-solve with your team to ensure it does not happen again in the future
D. Note the outcome in the change log and communicate it to your stakeholders
explanation: The correct sequence in the change control process is: approve the change, document it in the change log, and communicate the outcome to stakeholders first. Only after that should you elaborate the requirement into user stories, place it in the Sprint backlog, and then during the retrospective problem-solve to prevent recurrence. The question asks what to do next immediately after Change Control Board approval, which is to document and communicate the change.
topic: Change Control and Change Log Management

question: You are working on a hybrid project to replace an aging customer system that has just had a feature upgrade release. The senior developer raises a concern during the daily standup that the system doesn't seem to be able to connect to some parts of the organization after the upgrade. What will you do next?
options: A. Replan the project to ensure the correct scope is worked on that will connect to the organization
B. Assign it to a responsible party and work with them to find a resolution
C. Raise the concern as a risk and brainstorm risk responses
D. Escalate the issue to the technical managers so they can resolve it
explanation: This is an issue that has already occurred and is impacting the project, not a potential risk. The appropriate response is to assign the issue to a responsible party and work collaboratively to find a resolution. This follows the issue log process where issues are documented and assigned to an owner for resolution. While escalation to technical managers might eventually occur, the best practice is to assign to a responsible party and remove blockers to support resolution, rather than simply escalating.

question: You are working on an agile project where your project team has raised multiple risks to project delivery. Some of these risks may impact your cost and schedule significantly. Your program manager asks you to manage them using a risk-adjusted backlog. What will you do next?
options: A. Adjust your risk register to include the work not completed if the risks are triggered
B. Adjust your backlog velocity so that your end date is not impacted by the raised risks
C. Gather risk mitigation responses from your retrospectives and assign them to an owner
D. Place at least one risk response in each Sprint so you are managing and closing the risks
explanation: A risk-adjusted backlog is a backlog that includes both regular work and actions to address threats and opportunities. Risk responses should be included as work items in the backlog and prioritized alongside other work to be executed in Sprints. This ensures that risk mitigation is actively managed and completed as part of the sprint work, rather than being deferred or treated separately. This is the agile approach to managing risks as part of the regular backlog flow.
topic: Risk-Adjusted Backlog and Agile Risk Management

question: You are delivering a project to a functional area in your organization. The project is highly complex and there are many risks to project delivery. The functional manager would like to see the biggest risks that are assigned to her. What will you do next?
options: A. Show the risk-adjusted backlog
B. Show the risk register
C. Show the risk management plan
D. Show her the qualitative risk assessment
explanation: The risk register is the comprehensive document that contains all identified risks, their descriptions, assigned owners/responsible parties, risk responses, and mitigation strategies. Since the functional manager specifically wants to see the risks assigned to her, the risk register is the appropriate document to show, as it includes all this information in one place. The risk management plan describes the process for managing risks. The qualitative risk assessment shows impact and likelihood but not ownership. A risk-adjusted backlog is specific to agile projects and may not apply here since the project complexity is emphasized but agile is not mentioned.

question: Your project has been in progress for the past 6 months. Someone you have not met before approaches you and says they have not been engaged sufficiently, even though they believe their team is highly impacted by the change. They advise they may block the project as a result. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Perform stakeholder engagement assessment with your stakeholder
B. Add them to the stakeholder register including their salary and interest information
C. Add them to the stakeholder register including their impact and influence information
D. Raise a risk to the project delivery due to the nature of their actions
explanation: The stakeholder register records information about project stakeholders, including their assessment and classification. Since this stakeholder was not previously identified, they need to be added to the register with their impact and influence information so that their details are recorded and their classification is understood. While a risk could be raised, managing the stakeholder first through proper documentation is the better approach. Salary information is not appropriate for a stakeholder register.

question: You are a product owner on an agile project. You have created a product backlog of features in prioritized order for delivery. You ask the team to break down the first feature into user stories and estimate their effort. What will you do next?
options: A. Create an iteration plan with your team
B. Create the product roadmap with your team
C. Perform a Sprint review
D. Begin daily stand-ups with your team
explanation: After breaking down features into user stories with estimates, the next step is to create an iteration plan (Sprint plan) with your team. This plan matches the estimated work for the Sprint to the average Sprint velocity, determining how much work can fit into the upcoming iteration. The product roadmap would have been created earlier, Sprint reviews occur after work is completed, and daily stand-ups cannot begin until the Sprint is planned.

question: You have worked with your project customer to create a list of features that will bring the most value to them. They would like to see when some of this value will be delivered and the expected outcomes of each. What will you do next?
options: A. Show the features at the next Sprint review so the customer can see what they look like
B. Place the features on a release plan and show this to the customer
C. Check the features against the velocity chart to see when they might be delivered
D. Place the features into the next Sprint plan and show this to your customer
explanation: A release plan is the appropriate tool to show the customer when features will be delivered and the expected outcomes over multiple iterations. The release plan provides a forward-looking view of feature delivery and timelines, which is what the customer is asking for. A Sprint review only shows near-term deliverables, the velocity chart alone doesn't provide the scheduling view needed, and the next Sprint plan is too narrow in scope to show future value delivery.

question: You are in the planning stages of your project where you need to find out exactly what the project customer needs so you can find a solution and make it happen. Your business analysts are new to their roles and they are not sure what their process will be. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the business analysts to begin writing user stories
B. Create the project management plan to outline how the project will be delivered
C. Work with the business analyst to create a requirements management plan
D. Create a work breakdown structure on the project scope
explanation: The requirements management plan (also called the business analysis plan) describes the process for how requirements will be analyzed, documented, and managed. Since the business analysts are new and unsure of their process, creating this plan first establishes the methodology they will follow. Writing user stories prematurely skips the requirements gathering process, creating the overall project management plan is too broad, and the WBS comes after requirements are understood.

question: You are working on a new software application with a lot of complex interdependencies. The senior user providing and testing most of the requirements is concerned that some of those dependencies will cause errors upon release if they are not checked thoroughly. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the senior user for a written list of all their requirements and check if they are included in the project scope
B. Raise a risk on the software complexity with the project team
C. Create an affinity diagram with the senior user to group things together by their affinity to each other
D. Review the test plan with the senior user
explanation: The test plan is where requirements, test cases, acceptance criteria, and complex interdependencies are all captured and defined. Reviewing the test plan with the senior user ensures that all dependencies and critical test scenarios are included before release. This is where errors from interdependencies will be caught. A risk can be raised, but the test plan is the primary artifact for managing and validating complex testing scenarios.

question: You are planning your project and have captured the requirements from the project customer. You ask your team to plan the project scope, but they need to see exactly what needs to be delivered and how each part relates to each other. What will you do next?
options: A. Create a product breakdown structure with your team
B. Create the requirements traceability matrix with your project team
C. Create the risk breakdown structure with your project team
D. Create the scope management plan with your project team
explanation: A product breakdown structure is a hierarchical structure that reflects the product's components and deliverables in a whole-to-parts relationship, showing exactly how each part relates to the others. This visual representation shows what needs to be delivered and how everything is interconnected. The requirements traceability matrix maps requirements to deliverables but doesn't show the structural relationships as clearly. The risk breakdown structure and scope management plan serve different purposes.
topic: Scope Definition and Product Breakdown Structure

question: You have planned and begun the current Sprint where the project team have committed to delivering and releasing a particular feature. You need to see how the team are tracking towards their Sprint goal. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the Kanban board
B. Review the team velocity chart
C. Review the Sprint burndown chart
D. Perform a Sprint review with the team
explanation: A Sprint burndown chart is a graphical representation of the work remaining in a time box (iteration or Sprint) and compares the ideal trend with the items actually completed, making it the best tool to track progress towards the Sprint goal. A Kanban board shows work items but not burndown over time. A velocity chart measures capacity across Sprints rather than current Sprint progress. A Sprint review is performed after the Sprint is complete, not during it to track progress.

question: Your agile project has recently been merged into a larger program and the program manager is reviewing how the project is tracking. She asks you how much has been completed in design, but also how much has been completed in development, testing, and sign-off. What will you do next?
options: A. Show her the Sprint Kanban board
B. Take her through the team product backlog
C. Show her the team cumulative flow diagram
D. Show her the team velocity chart
explanation: A cumulative flow diagram indicates features completed over time separated at each stage (backlog, design, development, testing, sign-off, etc.), providing visibility into work distribution across all phases. A Kanban board shows only a single Sprint's work. A product backlog shows work items but not their phase distribution. A velocity chart measures capacity per Sprint, not work distribution across phases.

question: The features developed by your agile team have added some complexity to your website, delivered quickly with no time to refactor the code so it is a bit messy. You believe the user stories are moving slower now as a result but you cannot be sure. What will you do next?
options: A. Check the cycle time chart of the team
B. Shadow the developers to overview the work they are doing
C. View the product roadmap for proposed release dates
D. Review the points in the Sprint backlog versus the last three Sprints velocity
explanation: A cycle time chart shows the average cycle time of work items completed over time and can reveal if individual user stories are taking longer to complete due to code complexity. This directly measures how long it takes to complete each item from start to finish. Shadowing developers is not a data-driven approach. The product roadmap does not show item completion times. Comparing Sprint backlog points shows overall team velocity, not individual story cycle times.

question: You are working on an agile project with a full product roadmap for the next 6 months. Your project team are not sure what the next feature does or how they should turn it into user stories. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the scope Baseline
B. Perform story mapping with your team
C. Perform Sprint planning with your team
D. Perform a retrospective with your team
explanation: A story map is a visual model of all features and functionality desired for a given product, created to give the project team a holistic view of what they are building and why. It breaks features down into user stories based on functionality. This directly addresses the need to understand the feature and translate it into user stories. Sprint planning cannot occur until user stories are defined. A retrospective is held after a Sprint to improve processes, not to define requirements. A scope Baseline is more appropriate for traditional projects.

question: You are a program manager on a large-scale agile program where each project team has its own Scrum Master. You need to know the rate at which work is flowing through each team so you can see if there are any blockers or issues and whether they will remain on track. What will you do next?
options: A. Raise your query at Scrum of Scrums
B. Attend the Sprint review of each team at the end of each Sprint
C. Review the schedule management plan for managing the schedule
D. Review the team velocity charts
explanation: A velocity chart tracks the rate at which deliverables are produced, validated, and accepted within a predefined interval (Sprint) by showing what was committed versus what was completed. This metric directly shows the rate of work flowing through each team. Scrum of Scrums addresses coordination but not work rate metrics.
